exercises.title: Integer division and type casting
exercises.prompt: Java integer division truncates towards zero — there is no automatic promotion to double. Casting with (double) changes the type before division. What does this print (give all three lines)?
hints: 17 / 5 in Java truncates: 17 = 5*3 + 2, so the quotient is 3.
17 % 5 is the remainder: 2.
(double) a casts a to 3.4 before dividing, giving 3.4.
exercises.explanation: Key Java gotcha: int / int discards the fractional part silently. 17 / 5 = 3 (not 3.4). The % operator gives the remainder. Casting one operand to double forces floating-point division: (double) 17 / 5 = 3.4. Remember this for any Cambridge question involving division.

exercises.title: Write a Counter class with encapsulation
exercises.prompt: Write a Counter class demonstrating the three pillars of encapsulation: private fields (data hiding), public methods (interface), and a toString override. The field count should never be directly accessible from outside the class.
hints: this.count = 0 in the constructor (or just count = 0 since there's no shadowing).
increment: count++; or count += 1;
toString: return "Counter(" + count + ")";
exercises.explanation: Encapsulation: the private keyword hides count from external code — it can only be read via get() and modified via increment()/decrement(). This lets you enforce invariants (e.g. count >= 0). @Override tells the compiler you are intentionally overriding Object.toString() — the compiler will error if you misspell it.

exercises.title: Reference semantics and aliasing
exercises.prompt: In Java, variables of object/array types hold references (addresses), not copies. Assigning one reference to another makes both point to the same object. What does this print?
hints: b = a copies the reference, not the array. Both b and a point to the same memory.
Modifying b[1] changes the array that a also points to.
int y = x copies the primitive value 5 — changing y does not affect x.
exercises.explanation: Reference semantics is one of the most important Java concepts. Object variables store memory addresses. int[] b = a makes b point to the same array as a — so b[1] = 99 is visible through a[1] as well. Primitive types (int, double, boolean) are value types: int y = x copies the value, so subsequent changes to y do not affect x.

exercises.title: Method overriding and dynamic dispatch
exercises.prompt: When a subclass overrides a method, Java always calls the actual runtime type's version (dynamic dispatch). The declared type of the variable does not matter — the object's real class does. What does this print?
hints: s1 is declared as Shape but created as new Circle(). Which describe() is called?
Java looks at the runtime type (the actual object) when resolving method calls, not the declared type.
Dynamic dispatch: s1.describe() calls Circle.describe() because s1 IS a Circle at runtime.
exercises.explanation: Dynamic dispatch (runtime polymorphism) is the core mechanism of OOP. Even though s1 is declared as Shape, new Circle() creates a Circle object. Java looks up the method at runtime based on the actual object type — so Circle.describe() is called. This is why you can write methods that accept Shape and work correctly with any subclass.

exercises.title: Interfaces and polymorphic dispatch
exercises.prompt: Define a Drawable interface and implement it in two classes. Then write a method that draws any collection of Drawables — this is the key use of interfaces: define a contract that any class can implement, enabling polymorphism without inheritance.
hints: System.out.println("Circle r=" + radius); in Circle.draw()
System.out.println("Rectangle " + w + "x" + h); in Rectangle.draw()
drawAll uses the Drawable interface — it calls d.draw() without knowing which class d is.
exercises.explanation: Interfaces define a contract. Any class implementing Drawable must provide draw(). The drawAll method only knows about Drawable — it's decoupled from Circle and Rectangle. This is the open/closed principle: add new shapes without modifying drawAll. Contrast with inheritance: a class can implement multiple interfaces but only extend one class.

exercises.title: ArrayList sorting and Comparator
exercises.prompt: Collections.sort() sorts in natural order (lexicographic for strings). Comparator.reverseOrder() reverses it. ArrayList.get(i) is zero-indexed. What does this print?
hints: Natural alphabetical order: apple < banana < cherry < date. get(0) = apple, get(2) = cherry.
Reverse order: date > cherry > banana > apple. get(0) = date.
Sorted by length: date(4), apple(5), banana(6), cherry(6). get(0) = date.
exercises.explanation: Java's sort is stable and uses TimSort. Collections.sort(list) sorts ascending by natural order. list.sort(Comparator) sorts by a custom comparator. Comparator.comparingInt(f) creates a comparator based on an int-valued function. String::length is a method reference — equivalent to s -> s.length(). get(0) is the first (smallest after sort) element.

exercises.title: Write a generic Pair<A, B> class
exercises.prompt: Generics allow you to write type-safe containers and algorithms. Write a Pair<A, B> class that holds two values of potentially different types. This is a standard exam exercise for the Generics topic.
hints: In the constructor: this.first = first; this.second = second;
getFirst() returns first; getSecond() returns second.
swap() returns new Pair<>(second, first) — note the type parameters flip: Pair<B, A>.
exercises.explanation: Type parameters A and B are placeholders filled in at the call site: Pair<String, Integer> substitutes A=String, B=Integer. The compiler enforces this: p.getFirst() is known to return String without a cast. Generic classes avoid ClassCastException at runtime by catching type errors at compile time. final fields make Pair immutable — good practice for value objects.

exercises.title: Exception flow: try/catch/finally
exercises.prompt: Java exceptions propagate up the call stack until caught. The finally block always runs, even when an exception is thrown. What does this print, line by line?
hints: "A" prints, then the exception is thrown. "B" is never reached.
The first catch block catches IllegalArgumentException. Is RuntimeException a subtype? It's the other way round — check the hierarchy.
The second catch matches RuntimeException exactly. "D: oops" prints.
finally always runs. Then execution continues normally — "F" prints.
exercises.explanation: Exception hierarchy: RuntimeException extends Exception. IllegalArgumentException extends RuntimeException. The thrown exception IS a RuntimeException but IS NOT an IllegalArgumentException — so the first catch is skipped. Java checks catch blocks in order, matching the first compatible type. finally runs no matter what — even if an exception propagated out uncaught, finally would execute before propagation continued.

exercises.title: Factory Method pattern
exercises.prompt: The Factory Method pattern delegates object creation to a factory — the client doesn't use new directly. This decouples the client from concrete classes. Implement a simple AnimalFactory that creates Dogs or Cats by name, using an Animal interface.
hints: Use switch or if-else on type.toLowerCase().
Return new Dog() for "dog", new Cat() for "cat".
throw new IllegalArgumentException("Unknown: " + type); for unrecognised types.
exercises.explanation: The Factory Method pattern is on the OOP syllabus. Key benefit: the client code (main) asks for "dog" without knowing the Dog class exists — it only knows the Animal interface. To add Parrot, you extend AnimalFactory without touching Main. In Java 14+, switch expressions with -> make this cleaner, but if-else works fine too.

exercises.title: Stream pipelines with lambdas and method references
exercises.prompt: Java 8+ streams allow functional-style data processing: filter selects elements, map transforms them, collect gathers them, forEach consumes them. Lambdas (n -> n.startsWith("A")) and method references (String::toUpperCase) replace anonymous classes. Complete the pipeline.
hints: filter takes a Predicate: n -> n.startsWith("A")
map takes a Function: String::toUpperCase (method reference) or n -> n.toUpperCase()
Second filter: n -> n.length() > 4. Names with >4 chars: Alice(5), Andrew(6), Charlie(7), Anna(4 — NO).
exercises.explanation: Streams are lazy: nothing happens until a terminal operation (forEach, count, collect). The pipeline reads left to right: get stream → filter → map → sort → consume. Method references (String::toUpperCase) are shorthand for lambdas (s -> s.toUpperCase()). Streams don't modify the original list — they produce new values. The sorted names starting with A are: ALICE, ANDREW, ANNA. Names longer than 4: Alice(5), Andrew(6), Charlie(7) = 3.
